Gal 2:15-21.pericope_label: Not by Works of Law — Justified by Faith in/of Christ
Gal 2:15-21.literary_note: Galatians 2:15-21 is the theological core of Paul's rebuke of Peter at Antioch (2:11-14), functioning as either a direct speech to Peter or the beginning of Paul's theological argument. V.20 ('I have been crucified with Christ...') is possibly the most autobiographical verse in Paul's letters. The pericope contains the three hotly contested πίστις Χριστοῦ phrases (vv.16, 20) and the antithesis between νόμος (law) and πίστις (faith/faithfulness).

key_terms.note: δικαιωθῶμεν (justified, v.16): aorist passive subjunctive of δικαιόω — to be declared righteous. The same forensic verdict language as Romans 3-5. 'We know that a person is not justified by works of law but through πίστις Χριστοῦ.' The passive voice indicates God as the one who justifies.

πίστις Χριστοῦ (faith of/in Christ, vv.16, 20): The most debated genitive in NT scholarship. Objective genitive: 'faith in Christ' (traditional Protestant). Subjective genitive: 'the faithfulness of Christ' (Campbell, Hays). The subjective reading sees Christ's own covenant faithfulness (displayed in his obedient death) as the ground of justification, not merely the object of our trust. Both make theological sense; the Greek allows both.

συνεσταύρωμαι (I have been crucified with, v.20): Perfect passive — a completed action with ongoing state. Paul is speaking of his union with Christ's death through baptism (Rom 6:3-4). The 'old Paul' (defined by Torah identity, Pharisaic zeal) died in this co-crucifixion; the 'new Paul' lives by Christ's life within him.

Χριστὸς ἐν ἐμοί (Christ in me, v.20): The indwelling of Christ is not metaphor for Paul but the daily operative reality of Christian existence. This is the mystical union language that runs throughout Paul's letters (Col 1:27; Rom 8:10). 'I live, yet no longer I' — the I is retained but relativized by the prior reality of Christ's life.

cultural_notes: The Antioch Incident (2:11-14): Peter (Cephas) had been eating with Gentile believers but withdrew when 'certain people from James' arrived, apparently fearing criticism from the 'circumcision party.' Paul's rebuke was that Peter's behavior implicitly enforced Jewish food laws as a boundary marker for fellowship — making Torah observance a condition of full table membership.

Works of law as ethnic boundary markers: The 'New Perspective on Paul' (E.P. Sanders, James Dunn) argues that 'works of law' in Galatians refers specifically to the Torah practices that marked Israel off from Gentiles — circumcision, food laws, sabbath — not moral works in general. The Antioch context supports this: the conflict was over table fellowship, not morality.

Co-crucifixion and Roman shame: Crucifixion was the most degrading form of Roman execution, reserved for slaves and insurrectionists. Paul's embrace of the language of co-crucifixion (v.20) is the radical inversion of social honor: the most shameful death is the ground of new life and identity.
intertextual_connections: Rom 3:21-26 — the same justification by faith/faithfulness language; ἱλαστήριον connects to the mercy seat background that grounds 2:16.

Phil 3:7-9 — Paul's autobiography in brief: 'I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord... that I may be found in him, not having a righteousness of my own from the law, but that which comes through faith in Christ.'
Gal 3:1-14 — the argument continues: Abraham believed God and it was reckoned to him as righteousness (Gen 15:6) — faith, not circumcision or Torah, is the original covenant pathway.
2 Cor 5:14-17 — 'One died for all, therefore all died... if anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation.' The same co-death logic as Gal 2:20.
Gal 2:15-21.synthesis: Galatians 2:15-21 is the theological detonator of the entire letter. The Antioch incident is not primarily about social manners — it is about the gospel itself. If table fellowship requires Torah observance, then Christ is not sufficient; his death was unnecessary ('if righteousness comes through the law, then Christ died for nothing,' v.21). Paul's argument moves from the social (who can eat together) to the cosmological (what has Christ's death accomplished). The co-crucifixion language of v.20 is not mystical abstraction but the ontological claim that underwrites everything: the person defined by Torah identity is dead; the person alive in Christ is constituted by a different reality — 'Christ lives in me.' This is why the Antioch incident was not negotiable: to revert to Torah boundary-marking is to deny that the old defining structures are dead. It is to rebuild what was torn down (v.18).

tradition_map.patristic: Chrysostom's commentary on Galatians reads 2:20 as the key to Christian identity: the old 'I' is dead; we live by Christ's indwelling. Augustine emphasized the grace-against-works contrast as foundational for anti-Pelagian theology.
tradition_map.reformation: Luther's Galatians commentary (1535) is the Reformation's most impassioned work: 'This verse (2:20) is the foundation of all Paul's theology and the sum of Christian living.' For Luther, 2:15-21 is the answer to the terrified conscience — Christ lives in me, not my performance.
modern_debates: πίστις Χριστοῦ: Objective vs. subjective genitive. The traditional reading ('faith in Christ') grounds justification in our trust; the subjective reading ('Christ's faithfulness') grounds it in Christ's own covenant loyalty displayed in his death. Richard Hays' 1983 dissertation started the modern revival of the subjective reading; the debate remains unsettled.

New Perspective implications: If 'works of law' means ethnic boundary markers, then Galatians is not primarily about moralism vs. grace but about ethnic inclusion — Jews and Gentiles as one people without Torah-enforced distinction. Traditional Protestant readings resist this but it has deeply influenced modern scholarship.

V.20 and mysticism: Is Paul describing a unique apostolic experience or the universal Christian condition? The absence of 'I' qualifiers suggests Paul intends v.20 as a description of all who are baptized into Christ's death (cf. Rom 6).

Gal 3:10-14.pericope_label: Christ Redeemed Us from the Curse of the Law
Gal 3:10-14.literary_note: Gal 3:10-14 is the compact soteriological argument at the heart of Paul's Abraham exposition (3:1-29). It moves from diagnostic (those relying on works are under a curse — Deut 27:26) to solution (Christ became a curse for us — Deut 21:23) to result (the blessing of Abraham comes to the Gentiles). Two Deuteronomy citations govern the argument; the juxtaposition of Deut 27 and 21 is Paul's most precise exegetical move in the letter.

key_terms.note: κατάρα (curse, vv.10, 13): The covenantal curse — the consequence specified in Deut 27-28 for violating Torah. Paul's argument: the Torah itself pronounces a curse on all who fail to do everything it requires (27:26 LXX: 'everyone who does not abide by all things written in the book of the law'). This is not Paul's invention — it is from Deuteronomy.

ἐξηγόρασεν (redeemed, v.13): From ἀγοράζω (to buy in the marketplace). In commercial use, to purchase a slave's freedom. Christ's death is described as a purchase — he paid the price (the curse) so that the curse's hold on those under Torah is broken. Paul uses this vocabulary deliberately: freedom from bondage is the letter's main register.

ἐπικατάρατος πᾶς ὁ κρεμάμενος ἐπὶ ξύλου (cursed is everyone who hangs on a tree, v.13): Deut 21:23 LXX — the law's pronouncement on anyone executed by hanging on a tree (including crucifixion). Paul's audacity: he applies this curse-text to Christ's death. The cross was not just shameful — by Deuteronomy's own terms it made Christ cursed. This is the mechanism of redemption: he absorbed the curse-declaration that rightly falls on covenant violators.

cultural_notes: Deuteronomy 27-28 cursing and blessing: These chapters contain the covenant blessings and curses — the sanctions of the Sinai covenant. Israel's entire history can be read as the outworking of Deut 28: exile was the curse-fulfillment (Deut 28:64-68). Paul reads the cross as the comprehensive curse-absorber, ending the curse-logic that has dominated the Mosaic era.

Deut 21:23 in Jewish thought: The verse was already applied to crucifixion in the DSS (11QT64) — 'if a man has committed a crime punishable by death... you shall hang him on a tree.' The Targum adds that the hanging is itself a curse from God. Paul takes this traditional reading and applies it to Christ — not to shame him but to explain the mechanism of redemption.

Spirit as Abrahamic promise (v.14): The pericope ends with 'so that we might receive the promised Spirit.' The Spirit is the Abrahamic blessing extended to Gentiles — the fulfillment of Gen 12:3 ('in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed'). This links atonement directly to Pentecost.
intertextual_connections: Deut 27:26 — 'Cursed is everyone who does not abide by all things written in the book of the law.' Paul's citation sets up the curse-problem.
Deut 21:22-23 — the tree-hanging curse. The most audacious OT citation in Paul: applied to the cross.
Gen 12:3; 15:6 — the Abrahamic blessing promised to all nations through faith, fulfilled in Christ (Gal 3:14).
2 Cor 5:21 — 'God made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.' The same mechanism: Christ absorbs the negative verdict so we receive the positive one.
Gal 3:10-14.synthesis: The compressed argument of Gal 3:10-14 is Paul's most precise statement of substitutionary atonement. The diagnosis: Torah pronounces a curse on those who fail to keep all of it — a total claim no one meets (Deut 27:26). The solution: Christ became a curse in our place (Deut 21:23 applied to crucifixion). The result: the curse-logic is broken, and the blessing promised to Abraham and through him to all nations (Gen 12:3) flows freely by faith. The two Deuteronomy texts are not proof-texted out of context — they describe the real Torah structure (curse and blessing) and Christ's real positioning within that structure. The cross is not an accident of history but the precise mechanism by which the Mosaic covenant's curse-structure is absorbed and ended, opening the covenant to include Gentiles on the basis of faith, not Torah performance.

tradition_map.patristic: Irenaeus saw the 'curse becoming a blessing' as the recapitulation of Adam's failure — Christ reversing what Adam introduced. Chrysostom: the exchange of curse for blessing is pure grace, requiring nothing from us except faith.
tradition_map.reformation: Luther's 'joyful exchange' theology is rooted here: Christ takes our curse (sin, death, condemnation), we receive his blessing (righteousness, life, blessing). Calvin saw the divine justice satisfied and the blessing channel opened.
modern_debates: Does the Torah really pronounce a universal curse? Paul cites Deut 27:26 but quotes it more broadly than the Hebrew (which lists specific covenant violations). E.P. Sanders argued Israel was 'in the covenant' by grace, not threatened by curse for imperfection. Paul appears to be reading Deut 27-28 as pronouncing a comprehensive verdict.

Mechanism of atonement: Is v.13 penal substitution (Christ bears the punishment we deserved) or something like 'absorption' or 'identification'? The 'became a curse' language is stronger than 'bore our guilt' — it may suggest Christ entering into our condemned condition rather than simply receiving punishment in our place.
